_readme: Edit visibility per skill. Values: public | featured | preview | coming-soon | hidden. Optional publishAfter (ISO date) auto-promotes to public. Restart not needed — page reads this at load.

product.name: QIS-Skills
product.tagline: Senior QE judgment, installed into your editor

skills.name: Story Readiness
skills.tagline: Evaluates backlog items before sprint planning
skills.highlight: 60–70% less analysis toil on story intake
skills.description: Acts as a senior BA/QA lead: fetches the work item, auto-traverses the parent epic and sibling stories, checks regression memory, scans the codebase for impacted validation rules, and only asks questions it genuinely can't answer itself.
skills.howItWorks: The agent receives a work item ID, fetches the story from Azure DevOps, then automatically traverses the parent epic and all sibling stories to build context. It scans the codebase for validation rules and business logic affected by the change, checks the regression memory for related failures, and produces a structured readiness assessment — flagging gaps, ambiguous ACs, and missing dependencies before planning.
skills.whyItMatters: Story readiness is where most quality debt begins. When incomplete or ambiguous stories enter a sprint, the downstream cost is 5–10× higher — rework, misaligned test cases, missed edge cases. This skill front-loads that senior QA judgment into every sprint planning session, so even a junior engineer gets the same readiness analysis a 15-year veteran would produce.
skills.keyDiscipline: Never asks a question it can answer from the codebase or tracker. Traverses the full story hierarchy (parent epic → sibling stories) before surfacing gaps. Produces a structured impact rating, not a subjective opinion.
inputs: Work item ID (story, bug, spike, enhancement)
outputs: Structured readiness summary with impact rating

skills.name: Test Case Author
skills.tagline: Manual test cases and traceability from requirements
skills.highlight: Zero duplicate coverage — checks regression memory first
skills.description: Turns a refined story into a complete manual test package. Checks regression memory and unit-test coverage first, analyzes linked PRs, consults naming standards, and generates positive, negative, and edge-case scenarios.
skills.howItWorks: Before writing a single test case, the agent checks regression memory and existing unit-test coverage to avoid duplication. It then analyzes the story's acceptance criteria, scans linked PRs for code changes, and consults project naming standards. It generates positive, negative, boundary, and edge-case scenarios — each with preconditions, steps, expected results, and a traceability link back to the AC it validates.
skills.whyItMatters: Duplicate test coverage is one of the biggest hidden costs in QA orgs. Teams write overlapping tests because no one checks what already exists. This skill eliminates that waste by making regression memory and unit coverage its first checkpoint — so new test cases are always net-new value.
skills.keyDiscipline: Checks regression memory and unit-test coverage before generating any test case. Follows strict naming conventions from the project's standards. Every test case traces back to a specific acceptance criterion — no orphan tests.
inputs: Work item ID with acceptance criteria
Optional: linked PRs, existing regression suites
outputs: Traceability matrix mapping ACs to test cases

skills.name: E2E Automation Architect
skills.highlight: Discovers real locators — never invents selectors
skills.description: Transforms requirements into production-grade Playwright automation with mandatory discipline: verifies unit-test coverage, queries the tracker, discovers real locators from the codebase, and inventories page objects before writing new ones.
skills.howItWorks: The agent starts by verifying what dev unit tests already cover so it doesn't duplicate assertions. It queries the tracker for story context, then does a deep scan of the application codebase to discover real selectors — data-testid attributes, aria labels, form names. It inventories existing page objects and fixtures before creating anything new. Only then does it generate Playwright test files, page object models, and fixture setup — all wired into the existing project structure.
skills.whyItMatters: Most AI-generated automation is unusable because it invents selectors that don't exist in the real DOM. This skill's mandatory locator-discovery step means every selector it writes is grounded in actual source code. The result is automation that passes on first run, not automation that looks good in a demo but fails in CI.
skills.keyDiscipline: Mandatory pre-flight: never invents selectors. Discovers data-testid, aria-label, and form attributes from the actual codebase. Inventories existing page objects so new tests integrate cleanly instead of creating parallel structures.
outputs: Playwright suites with Page Object Models
Fixtures, test data setup, test plan

skills.name: PR Quality Gate
skills.highlight: Six gates: assertion honesty, determinism, traceability, scope hygiene
skills.description: Reviews UI/API test-automation pull requests against six gates. Asks for human approval before posting anything to the PR — actionable fixes only, no style nitpicks.
skills.howItWorks: The agent checks every test file in the PR diff against six quality gates: assertion honesty (are you actually asserting what you claim?), determinism (no random sleeps, flaky waits), test data isolation, traceability to stories, coverage scope, and naming hygiene. It drafts review comments per finding, then asks the reviewer to approve before posting — so nothing touches the PR without human sign-off.
skills.whyItMatters: Bad test-automation PRs are the number one source of flaky pipelines. Teams merge tests that look correct but have subtle determinism issues, phantom assertions, or orphaned data. This skill catches those patterns before merge — blocking on the ones that matter, advisory on the rest.
skills.keyDiscipline: Never posts to the PR without human approval. Separates blocking findings (assertion honesty, determinism) from advisory ones (naming, style). No style nitpicks — only actionable, fix-it-now findings.
inputs: Optional: linked story for coverage checking

skills.name: Test Evidence Linker
skills.tagline: Closes the loop between automation and the tracker
skills.highlight: Full evidence chain — video, screenshots, bugs auto-filed
skills.description: Runs Playwright tests, captures artifacts, uploads to the tracker, sets Associated Automation fields, posts result comments to stories, and files structured bugs on failure.
skills.howItWorks: After Playwright tests run, the agent collects all artifacts — videos, screenshots, trace files, console logs. It uploads these to the tracker as evidence, links the automation run to manual test cases via Associated Automation fields, and posts a result summary comment on the story. On failure, it auto-files a structured bug with full evidence, repro steps, and traceability links back to the failing test and story.
skills.whyItMatters: The gap between 'automation ran' and 'everyone can see what happened' is where visibility dies. Stakeholders check the tracker, not CI logs. This skill closes that loop automatically — every test run produces traceable evidence where decision-makers actually look.
skills.keyDiscipline: Full evidence chain: every automation run produces attached artifacts, tracker links, and — on failure — a structured bug with repro steps. No manual copy-paste between CI and the tracker.
inputs: Playwright test files or a test run
Manual test case and story IDs to link
outputs: Auto-filed bugs with evidence on failure

skills.name: Defect Triage
skills.highlight: 10-step runbook a junior engineer can follow
skills.description: Classifies every issue through a decision tree, gathers evidence, writes reproducible steps, assigns severity, and files a complete work item with traceability links.
skills.howItWorks: The agent follows a 10-step decision tree: classify the issue type (in-sprint bug vs. post-sprint defect vs. production incident), determine severity using a structured rubric, gather evidence (logs, screenshots, reproduction steps), write clear repro steps that anyone can follow, assign to the correct area path and iteration, and file a complete work item with all traceability links. The result is a defect that's ready for developer investigation on first read.
skills.whyItMatters: Poorly filed defects waste developer time — they bounce back for clarification, get mis-prioritized, or sit in triage limbo. This skill ensures every defect is filed with enough context that a developer can reproduce and investigate without a single follow-up question.
skills.keyDiscipline: Structured decision tree: never guesses severity — uses a rubric. Every defect includes repro steps a developer can follow independently. Classification separates in-sprint bugs from post-sprint defects from production incidents.
inputs: Failure description, logs, or failing test evidence
outputs: Repro steps, severity triage, environment details

skills.name: Accessibility Guardian
skills.tagline: WCAG / Section 508 audits and conformance reports
skills.highlight: DHS Trusted Tester v5.1 discipline built in
skills.description: Runs automated accessibility audits mapped to the DHS Trusted Tester process. Performs static analysis on HTML/JSX/TSX and produces VPAT-style conformance reporting.
skills.howItWorks: The agent scans the target page or component source against WCAG 2.1/2.2 success criteria, following the DHS Trusted Tester v5.1 methodology. It checks semantic HTML structure, ARIA attribute correctness, color contrast ratios, keyboard navigability, and form label associations. It produces a prioritized findings list with code-level fix recommendations and a VPAT-style conformance report that maps each finding to the relevant WCAG criterion and Section 508 requirement.
skills.whyItMatters: Accessibility issues found after deployment cost 10× more to fix and carry legal risk. The DHS Trusted Tester process is the federal standard — having it built into every code review means accessibility is tested with the same rigor as functional correctness, not bolted on as an afterthought.
skills.keyDiscipline: Maps every finding to a specific WCAG success criterion and Trusted Tester test rule — no generic 'fix accessibility' recommendations. Produces a VPAT-format report suitable for procurement and compliance review.
inputs: A URL, page, or HTML/JSX/TSX source

skills.name: API Acceptance Architect
skills.tagline: .NET MSTest API acceptance suites with standard patterns
skills.highlight: Success, negative, and security cases per endpoint
skills.description: Creates or refactors .NET API acceptance-test suites with a standardized scenario contract per endpoint, mandatory pre-flight checks, and dev-coverage gap callouts.
skills.howItWorks: For each API endpoint, the agent generates a standardized scenario contract: success cases, negative/error cases, and security boundary tests. It creates or extends the .NET MSTest project with async HTTP client patterns, shared test data setup, and environment configuration. Before generating tests, it checks existing developer unit tests and integration tests for coverage overlap — and calls out gaps where dev coverage is missing.
skills.whyItMatters: API acceptance testing without a standard contract is ad hoc — teams test the happy path and miss error codes, auth boundaries, and rate limits. This skill enforces a complete contract per endpoint so coverage is consistent across the team, not dependent on who wrote the test.
skills.keyDiscipline: Standardized scenario contract per endpoint: success, negative, and security cases — no endpoint ships without all three. Checks dev coverage first to avoid duplication and surface gaps.
outputs: Standard project structure with async client
Coverage gap report vs dev-owned tests

skills.name: API Test Modernizer
skills.tagline: Legacy .NET API test projects → modern architecture
skills.highlight: Gated migration with before/after parity verification
skills.description: Migrates legacy .NET API acceptance-test projects through intake, feasibility gate, runtime readiness gate, then stepwise upgrade with parity verification.
skills.howItWorks: The migration runs through four gates: Intake (inventory the legacy project — framework version, dependencies, test count), Feasibility (can it be upgraded in-place or does it need a rewrite?), Runtime Readiness (can the existing suite run green before migration?), and Stepwise Upgrade (target framework update, async HTTP client migration, dependency cleanup). After each gate, a parity report verifies that the modernized suite produces equivalent results to the original.
skills.whyItMatters: Legacy test projects are a ticking time bomb — unsupported frameworks, synchronous HTTP calls, deprecated dependencies. But migrating them manually is error-prone and risky. This gated approach means you never break a working suite — every step is verified for parity before proceeding.
skills.keyDiscipline: Gated migration: no step proceeds without parity verification against the previous state. Before/after test counts and pass rates must match before the next gate opens.
inputs: Access to run the suite for parity
outputs: Modernized project on latest target framework
Async HTTP client and cleaned dependencies

skills.name: Load Test Architect
skills.highlight: Blazemeter Taurus / BZT with parameterization and chaining
skills.description: Builds API load-test scripts from OpenAPI spec automatically or manually. Handles environment config, test-data strategy, and pre-execution validation.
skills.howItWorks: The agent parses an OpenAPI/Swagger spec to extract endpoints, parameters, and response schemas. It generates Taurus/BZT YAML scripts with proper parameterization, request chaining (e.g., auth token → API call), and environment-specific configuration. It creates load, stress, and soak profiles with ramp-up patterns, and produces a pre-execution checklist to validate environment readiness, test data availability, and monitoring setup before the first run.
skills.whyItMatters: Load testing is frequently skipped or done poorly because the setup effort is too high. Teams run a single load profile and call it done. This skill generates production-ready scripts with multiple profiles (load, stress, soak) and pre-execution validation — so performance testing is consistent and repeatable, not a one-off.
skills.keyDiscipline: Pre-execution validation: never runs a load test without confirming environment readiness, test data availability, and monitoring setup. Generates multiple profiles (load/stress/soak), not just a single run.

skills.name: RUM Test Strategist
skills.tagline: Production RUM data → data-driven test strategy
skills.highlight: Real user flows drive coverage priorities
skills.description: Turns real-user-monitoring behavior into test strategy: discovers what users actually do, formulates coverage priorities, defines KPI gates on Web Vitals.
skills.howItWorks: The agent ingests RUM data (e.g., Datadog RUM sessions), analyzes real user behavior patterns — most-visited pages, common click paths, device/browser distributions, and error hotspots. It formulates a data-driven test strategy that prioritizes the flows real users actually take, defines KPI gates on Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS), and generates test cases targeting the highest-traffic, highest-risk paths first.
skills.whyItMatters: Most test strategies are based on assumptions about what users do. RUM data shows what they actually do — and the gap is always surprising. This skill ensures test coverage is aligned with real usage patterns, not product team guesses.
skills.keyDiscipline: Data-driven prioritization: test coverage is ordered by real user traffic and error frequency, not by story order or developer intuition. KPI gates on Core Web Vitals ensure performance is a first-class quality criterion.
outputs: Data-driven test strategy from real flows
KPI gates on Core Web Vitals
Generated test cases and coverage report

skills.name: Incident RCA Bridge
skills.tagline: Incident → root-cause analysis → tracker work item
skills.description: Fetches an incident, performs structured RCA using 5-Whys, Ishikawa, and timeline reconstruction — then creates a detailed tracker work item with full traceability.
skills.howItWorks: The agent fetches the incident details, reconstructs a timeline of events from logs and alerts, then performs structured root-cause analysis using three methods: 5-Whys (to drill past symptoms to the root cause), Ishikawa/fishbone diagram (to categorize contributing factors), and timeline correlation (to identify the triggering event). It produces an RCA document and files a detailed tracker work item with the root cause, corrective actions, and traceability links between the incident and the resulting work.
skills.whyItMatters: Post-incident RCA is the most important feedback loop in software delivery — and the one teams most often skip or rush. Without structured RCA, the same root causes recur across sprints. This skill makes RCA a 15-minute process instead of a half-day meeting.
skills.keyDiscipline: Three complementary RCA methods: 5-Whys for depth, Ishikawa for breadth, and timeline for sequence. Never stops at the first plausible explanation — drills until the systemic root cause is identified.
inputs: Access to incident tooling and tracker

skills.name: Legacy Parity Analyst
skills.highlight: Risk-rated parity gaps with QA test strategy
skills.description: Auto-discovers behavior in both legacy and modern codebases, produces gap analysis with risk assessment, and hands QA a test strategy focused on the riskiest parity gaps.
skills.howItWorks: The agent scans both the legacy and modern codebases to discover behavior — API contracts, validation rules, business logic, UI flows. It produces a side-by-side parity analysis highlighting what's missing, what's changed, and what's been added in the modern system. Each gap is risk-rated (critical, high, medium, low) based on business impact, and the output includes a QA test strategy focused on the highest-risk parity gaps.
skills.whyItMatters: Re-platforming projects fail most often because parity gaps go undetected until users hit them in production. Manual gap analysis is exhaustive and error-prone. This automated approach ensures every behavioral difference is documented and risk-rated before QA begins testing.
skills.keyDiscipline: Risk-rated gaps: not all parity differences matter equally. Critical business logic changes are separated from cosmetic differences. QA test strategy targets the riskiest gaps first.
outputs: QA test strategy for modernized system

skills.name: Mobile Automation
skills.description: The shared foundation for platform-specific mobile skills: Robot Pattern + Page Object Model conventions, locator discovery from codebase, framework file inventory.
skills.howItWorks: The agent scans the mobile app repository to discover real locators — TestTags for Compose, accessibility identifiers for iOS, resource IDs for XML layouts. It inventories existing page objects and robot classes before creating new ones. It generates test infrastructure following the Robot Pattern + Page Object Model: robots handle user actions, page objects encapsulate element discovery, and test classes orchestrate scenarios. Everything integrates into the existing project structure.
skills.whyItMatters: Mobile automation is fragile because locators change with every UI update. The Robot Pattern + POM architecture isolates locator changes to a single layer, so when the UI changes, you update one page object instead of 50 test files. And by discovering locators from real code, the automation works on first run.
skills.keyDiscipline: Locator discovery from real codebase: never assumes a TestTag or accessibility identifier exists — verifies by scanning source code. Inventories existing framework files to avoid duplicate page objects.
outputs: Page objects and robots matching conventions
Test classes wired into existing framework

skills.name: Android Automation
skills.tagline: Android UI tests in Kotlin (Compose / AndroidJUnit4)
skills.description: Generates Android UI tests using Jetpack Compose UI Test and AndroidJUnit4 with Robot Pattern + POM. Discovers TestTags from the actual codebase.
skills.howItWorks: The agent scans Kotlin source files for Modifier.testTag() calls and contentDescription attributes, building a map of discoverable UI elements. It generates Kotlin UI test classes using Jetpack Compose UI Test (ComposeTestRule) or AndroidJUnit4 with Espresso, following the Robot Pattern — one robot per screen, one page object per component, and test classes that read like user stories.
skills.whyItMatters: Android UI testing adoption is low because writing Compose UI tests requires deep knowledge of ComposeTestRule, semantic matchers, and test synchronization. This skill lowers the bar to 'give me a scenario' and produces tests that follow the team's existing conventions.
skills.keyDiscipline: TestTags discovered from real Kotlin source code — never hardcoded or assumed. Tests follow existing project conventions for naming, directory structure, and test runner configuration.
outputs: Robots and page objects from discovered TestTags

skills.name: iOS Automation
skills.tagline: iOS UI tests in Swift (XCUITest / XCTest)
skills.description: Generates iOS UI tests in Swift using XCUITest/XCTest with Robot Pattern + POM. Discovers accessibility identifiers from the real codebase.
skills.howItWorks: The agent scans Swift source files for .accessibilityIdentifier() calls and accessibilityLabel attributes, building a map of discoverable UI elements. It generates Swift XCUITest test classes following the Robot Pattern — one robot per screen, one page object per component, and tests that read like user stories with clear arrange/act/assert structure.
skills.whyItMatters: iOS UI test suites suffer from the same fragility as Android — hardcoded identifiers that break on UI changes. This skill grounds every test in real accessibility identifiers from the codebase and structures tests for maintainability with the Robot Pattern.
skills.keyDiscipline: Accessibility identifiers discovered from real Swift source code — never assumed. Tests integrate into the existing XCTest target and follow project directory conventions.
outputs: Robots and page objects from discovered identifiers

skills.name: Plan Interrogator
skills.tagline: Relentless plan interrogation until shared understanding
skills.highlight: Resolves every branch of the decision tree
skills.description: Interviews you about a plan or design the way a sharp architect would — surfacing unstated assumptions and refusing vague answers until genuine shared understanding is reached.
skills.howItWorks: The agent takes your plan, design doc, or rough idea and interrogates it through a structured decision tree. It identifies unstated assumptions, ambiguous requirements, missing error handling, and incomplete rollback strategies. It refuses vague answers ('we'll handle that later') and pushes until every branch of the decision tree has a concrete resolution. The output is a plan you can defend in a design review.
skills.whyItMatters: Most plans fail not because of technical mistakes but because of unstated assumptions. 'We assumed the API would handle that' or 'we didn't think about the failure case.' This skill surfaces those gaps before a line of code is written — when the cost of changing direction is near zero.
skills.keyDiscipline: Refuses vague answers: 'we'll figure it out later' is not accepted. Every assumption must be stated explicitly. Every decision branch must have a resolution before the interrogation ends.
inputs: A plan, design doc, or rough idea
outputs: A plan you can actually defend

skills.name: Diagram Forge
skills.tagline: Styled, animated Mermaid diagrams of any kind
skills.highlight: Flowchart, sequence, C4, ER, timeline, mindmap
skills.description: Creates, styles, and animates Mermaid diagrams with consistent styling standards, as standalone .mmd files or embedded Markdown blocks.
skills.howItWorks: The agent takes a description of a process, system, or data flow and generates a publication-quality Mermaid diagram. It applies consistent styling — color themes, spacing, node shapes — and supports flowcharts, sequence diagrams, C4 architecture diagrams, ER diagrams, timelines, and mindmaps. Output is a standalone .mmd file or an embedded Markdown block ready for documentation.
skills.whyItMatters: Good diagrams are essential for architecture documentation but time-consuming to create. This skill produces consistent, styled diagrams from natural language descriptions — so documentation stays current instead of going stale because 'nobody has time to update the diagrams.'
skills.keyDiscipline: Consistent styling standards across all diagram types. Supports six diagram types with proper Mermaid syntax validation. Output is publication-ready, not draft-quality.
inputs: A description of the process, system, or flow

skills.name: UI Craftsman
skills.highlight: WCAG 2.1/2.2 and Section 508 from the first line
skills.description: Builds distinctive, enterprise-grade frontend interfaces with accessibility built in from the first line, not bolted on after.
skills.howItWorks: The agent takes a UI requirement, design mockup, or verbal description and generates production-ready frontend components. It applies enterprise design patterns — consistent spacing, typography scales, color contrast — with WCAG 2.1/2.2 and Section 508 compliance built into the first line of code. Semantic HTML, ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, and focus management are not afterthoughts — they're the foundation.
skills.whyItMatters: Most UI code treats accessibility as a checklist to pass after the fact. Retrofitting accessibility is 3–5× more expensive than building it in. This skill produces components where accessibility is structural, not decorative.
skills.keyDiscipline: Accessibility-first architecture: semantic HTML and ARIA attributes are the foundation, not a bolt-on. Every component meets WCAG 2.1 AA from the first line — color contrast, keyboard nav, focus management.
inputs: UI requirement, design intent, or mockup
